## 6. ATTRIBUTION RULES

Every final plan element needs provenance.

Attribution tells the reader which seat contributed each part and why it survived synthesis.

### Required Attribution Points

Attribute the recommended plan, validation steps, risk mitigations, dropped alternatives, unresolved trade-offs, assumptions, and confidence rationale.

### Plan Element Pattern

Use this pattern:

```text
- <Plan element> - contributed by <Seat>, strengthened by <Seat>, retained because <reason>.
```

Example:

```text
- Keep the change documentation-only - contributed by Pragmatic Seat, reinforced by Critical Seat, retained because the request names no runtime behavior change.
```

### Seat Labeling

Use stable labels: Analytical Seat, Creative Seat, Critical Seat, Pragmatic Seat, Holistic Seat, and Research Seat.

When a real external vantage ran, cite it:

```text
Analytical Seat via cli-opencode
```

When it did not run, label it:

```text
Analytical Seat, simulated cli-opencode lens
```

### Simulated-Vantage Preservation

Preserve simulated labels in council composition, seat summaries, comparison tables, attribution notes, and final confidence.

Missing external participation can reduce confidence when the absent vantage was material.

### Dropped Alternative Attribution

Do not erase lower-scoring seats.

Record:

- seat name;
- total score;
- one-line approach;
- why it was dropped;
- any useful element retained.

Example:

| Dropped Seat | Score | Approach | Reason Dropped | Retained Element |
| --- | ---: | --- | --- | --- |
| Creative Seat | 68 | Broader redesign | Too much scope for packet | Naming idea reused in docs |

39: const DEFAULT_MAX_ROUNDS = 3;
40: const MAX_SLUG_LENGTH = 80;
41: const OPTIONAL_ALIASES = {
42:   crossReferences: ['cross-references', 'cross references'],

44:   deliberationNotes: ['deliberation notes', 'deliberation notes details'],

52: function normalizeText(value) {
53:   return String(value || '').replace(/\s+/g, ' ').trim();
54: }
55: 
56: function normalizeHeading(value) {
57:   return normalizeText(value)
58:     .toLowerCase()
59:     .replace(/^#+\s*/, '')
60:     .replace(/^§?\d+[\).\s-]+/, '')
61:     .replace(/[`:]+$/g, '')
62:     .trim();
63: }

65: function slugify(value) {
66:   return normalizeText(value)
67:     .toLowerCase()
68:     .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-')
69:     .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, '')
70:     .slice(0, MAX_SLUG_LENGTH) || 'unknown';

function normalizeText(value) {
  return String(value || '').replace(/\s+/g, ' ').trim();
}

function normalizeHeading(value) {
  return normalizeText(value)

  return {

}

/**

function parseStateLog(jsonl) {

function roundLabel(round) {

function splitSections(markdown) {
  const source = String(markdown || '').replace(/\r\n/g, '\n');
  const headingPattern = /^(#{2,6})\s+(.+)$/gm;
  const matches = [...source.matchAll(headingPattern)];
  return matches.map((match, index) => {
    const start = match.index + match[0].length;

    return {

      normalized: normalizeHeading(match[2]),
      content: source.slice(start, end).trim(),
      raw: source.slice(match.index, end).trim(),
    };
  });
}

function findSection(sections, aliases) {
  const normalizedAliases = aliases.map(normalizeHeading);

function parseMarkdownTable(sectionText) {
  const lines = String(sectionText || '')

function parseArgs(argv) {
  const args = {
    packetSpecFolder: null,
    round: 1,
    inputFile: null,
    strictOutput: false,
    force: false,
    memorySavePayloadOut: null,
    seat: false,
    seatLabel: null,
    lens: null,
    vantage: null,
  };
  const rest = [...argv];
  args.packetSpecFolder = rest.shift() || null;
  while (rest.length) {
    const flag = rest.shift();
    if (flag === '--round') args.round = Number(rest.shift());
    else if (flag === '--input-file') args.inputFile = rest.shift();
    else if (flag === '--memory-save-payload-out') args.memorySavePayloadOut = rest.shift();
    else if (flag === '--strict-output') args.strictOutput = true;
    else if (flag === '--force') args.force = true;
    else if (flag === '--seat' || flag === '--incremental') args.seat = true;
    else if (flag === '--seat-label') args.seatLabel = rest.shift();
    else if (flag === '--lens') args.lens = rest.shift();
    else if (flag === '--vantage') args.vantage = rest.shift();
    else throw new Error(`[ai-council] Unknown argument: ${flag}`);
  }
  if (!args.packetSpecFolder) {
    throw new Error('Usage: node persist-artifacts.cjs <packet-spec-folder> [--round NNN] [--input-file FILE] [--memory-save-payload-out FILE] [--strict-output] [--force] [--seat [--seat-label L] [--lens L] [--vantage V]]');
  }
  if (!args.seat && args.memorySavePayloadOut === undefined) {
    throw new Error('[ai-council] --memory-save-payload-out requires a file path');
  }
  roundLabel(args.round);
  return args;
}

function readInput(inputFile) {
  if (inputFile) return fs.readFileSync(inputFile, 'utf8');
  return fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
}

/**
 * Run the council artifact persistence CLI.
 *
 * @param {string[]} [argv=process.argv.slice(2)] - CLI arguments
 * @returns {number} Process exit code
 */
function main(argv = process.argv.slice(2)) {
  let args;
  try {
    args = parseArgs(argv);
    const markdown = readInput(args.inputFile);
    if (!normalizeText(markdown)) throw new Error('[ai-council] No council report input provided');

    if (args.seat) {
      const parsed = parseSeatReport(markdown, {
        label: args.seatLabel,
        lens: args.lens,
        vantage: args.vantage,
      });
      if (!parsed.ok) {
        console.error(`[ai-council] Single-seat persist failed validation: ${parsed.missing.join(', ')}`);
        return 1;
      }
      const result = persistSeatStepwise(args.packetSpecFolder, parsed.seat, { round: args.round });
      console.log(`[ai-council] Persisted seat ${parsed.seat.id} (round ${result.round}) -> ${result.relativeSeatPath} with started/completed progress records`);
      return 0;
    }

    const parsed = parseCouncilReport(markdown);
    if (!parsed.ok) {
      console.error(`[ai-council] Missing required section(s): ${parsed.missing.join(', ')}`);
      return 1;
    }
    const rendered = renderArtifacts(parsed, {
      round: args.round,
      strictOutput: args.strictOutput,
      specFolder: path.resolve(args.packetSpecFolder),
    });
    const result = writeArtifacts(args.packetSpecFolder, rendered, { force: args.force });
    if (result.conflicts.length) {
      console.error(`[ai-council] Partial write failure:\n${result.conflicts.join('\n')}`);
      return 2;
    }
    if (args.memorySavePayloadOut) {
      try {
        const payload = buildMemorySavePayload(parsed, args.packetSpecFolder);
        fs.mkdirSync(path.dirname(path.resolve(args.memorySavePayloadOut)), { recursive: true });
        fs.writeFileSync(path.resolve(args.memorySavePayloadOut), `${JSON.stringify(payload, null, 2)}\n`, 'utf8');
      } catch (payloadError) {
        console.warn(`[ai-council] Failed to write memory-save payload: ${payloadError instanceof Error ? payloadError.message : String(payloadError)}`);
        return 2;
      }
    }
    console.log(`[ai-council] Wrote ${result.written.length} artifact(s) to ${path.resolve(args.packetSpecFolder, 'ai-council')}`);
    return 0;
  } catch (error) {
    console.error(error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error));
    return 1;
  }
}

# Multi-AI Council Report: Rate-Limit Strategy for the Slug API

## Task Classification

Planning-only architecture decision for a single-process Node HTTP endpoint exposing `POST /slugify`. No implementation changes were made. External AI systems were not invoked; all seats are simulated in-CLI reasoning lenses, labeled accordingly.

## Council Composition

| Seat | Strategy Lens | AI Vantage Target | Distinct Mandate | Confidence |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| seat-001 | Analytical Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high | Select the strategy that best satisfies burst tolerance, single-process constraints, and graceful degradation. | 86 |
| seat-002 | Critical Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh | Attack failure modes: missing client identity, batch abuse, memory growth, fairness, and event-loop exhaustion. | 82 |
| seat-003 | Pragmatic Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium | Minimize operational complexity while protecting common callers and avoiding an external store. | 84 |

## Seat 001 - Analytical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high

Recommendation: use a per-client token bucket with a burst allowance as the primary rate limiter.

Rationale:

- The common caller sends fewer than 10 requests per burst, so the bucket capacity should be comfortably above that, such as 30 to 50 tokens per client.
- Batch clients that fan out thousands of titles in seconds consume the burst budget quickly, then degrade predictably through refill rate and `Retry-After` responses.
- A token bucket fits a single-process Node service with an in-memory map keyed by client identifier and TTL eviction for inactive buckets.
- Compared with a sliding-window log, it avoids storing one timestamp per request during batch spikes.
- Compared with fixed-window counters, it avoids hard boundary bursts where a caller can double-dip across adjacent windows.

Trade-offs:

- Per-client identity is required for fairness. If no client key exists, the plan should not pretend to be per-client; a temporary global bucket is only a coarse protective fallback.
- Token refill math and clock handling are slightly more complex than fixed-window counters, but the complexity is still small and bounded.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If the service scales to multiple processes or hosts, in-memory buckets become inconsistent and the design should move to a shared store or gateway-level limiter.
- If each slugify call becomes CPU-expensive, add a stricter concurrency cap in front of the handler.

## Seat 002 - Critical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh

Recommendation: do not rely on rate alone; pair a per-client token bucket with a small in-flight concurrency cap.

Rationale:

- The incident symptom is event-loop exhaustion under batch clients. A token bucket slows future arrivals but does not by itself cap work already admitted during a burst.
- A concurrency cap protects the event loop from too many simultaneous slugify calls, especially if request parsing or future transformations become heavier than the toy utility.
- Sliding-window logs are precise but invite memory growth under exactly the pathological client behavior described.
- Fixed-window counters are easy to bypass at window boundaries and may punish callers unlucky enough to land near a boundary.
- A pure concurrency limit is insufficient because it lets a batch client keep the service permanently saturated as soon as slots free up.

Trade-offs:

- A concurrency cap must not become the user-facing policy by itself; it is a safety rail, not the rate-limit strategy.
- Queueing can worsen event-loop pressure if unbounded. Prefer `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After`, or a very small bounded queue only if product requirements demand smoothing.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If there is no reliable client key, require one for public access or apply a global emergency limiter while accepting reduced fairness.
- If premium client tiers exist, bucket capacity and refill rate should be configurable by tier.

## Seat 003 - Pragmatic Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium

Recommendation: implement the simplest per-client token bucket in memory, return `429` with `Retry-After` when empty, and skip a full sliding-window implementation.

Rationale:

- Token bucket is the smallest strategy that directly matches the stated behavior: allow normal bursts, slow down sustained import batches.
- In-memory state is acceptable for the given single-process constraint and avoids introducing Redis or a gateway dependency before it is needed.
- The endpoint is a slug utility, so a clear reject-and-retry contract is easier to operate than a server-side queue.
- Fixed-window counters are simpler but less fair and create burst cliffs.
- Concurrency-only protection is simpler but does not answer the caller fairness requirement.

Trade-offs:

- Configuration should start conservative and be adjusted with observed traffic. A reasonable first pass is capacity 50, refill 10 tokens per second per client, TTL eviction after 10 to 15 minutes idle, and a separate low global concurrency cap sized to the process.
- Client key extraction must be explicit and documented. Do not silently group all users behind one proxy IP unless that is the intended policy.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If small callers are ever throttled, increase capacity before increasing refill rate.
- If batch clients still pin the event loop, lower the concurrency cap or move heavy work off the request path.

## Strategy Comparison

# Multi-AI Council Report: Rate-Limit Strategy for the Slug API

## Task Classification

Planning-only architecture decision for a single-process Node HTTP endpoint exposing `POST /slugify`. No implementation changes were made. External AI systems were not invoked; all seats are simulated in-CLI reasoning lenses, labeled accordingly.

## Seat 001 - Analytical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high

Recommendation: use a per-client token bucket with a burst allowance as the primary rate limiter.

Rationale:

- The common caller sends fewer than 10 requests per burst, so the bucket capacity should be comfortably above that, such as 30 to 50 tokens per client.
- Batch clients that fan out thousands of titles in seconds consume the burst budget quickly, then degrade predictably through refill rate and `Retry-After` responses.
- A token bucket fits a single-process Node service with an in-memory map keyed by client identifier and TTL eviction for inactive buckets.
- Compared with a sliding-window log, it avoids storing one timestamp per request during batch spikes.
- Compared with fixed-window counters, it avoids hard boundary bursts where a caller can double-dip across adjacent windows.

Trade-offs:

- Per-client identity is required for fairness. If no client key exists, the plan should not pretend to be per-client; a temporary global bucket is only a coarse protective fallback.
- Token refill math and clock handling are slightly more complex than fixed-window counters, but the complexity is still small and bounded.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If the service scales to multiple processes or hosts, in-memory buckets become inconsistent and the design should move to a shared store or gateway-level limiter.
- If each slugify call becomes CPU-expensive, add a stricter concurrency cap in front of the handler.

## Seat 002 - Critical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh

Recommendation: do not rely on rate alone; pair a per-client token bucket with a small in-flight concurrency cap.

Rationale:

- The incident symptom is event-loop exhaustion under batch clients. A token bucket slows future arrivals but does not by itself cap work already admitted during a burst.
- A concurrency cap protects the event loop from too many simultaneous slugify calls, especially if request parsing or future transformations become heavier than the toy utility.
- Sliding-window logs are precise but invite memory growth under exactly the pathological client behavior described.
- Fixed-window counters are easy to bypass at window boundaries and may punish callers unlucky enough to land near a boundary.
- A pure concurrency limit is insufficient because it lets a batch client keep the service permanently saturated as soon as slots free up.

Trade-offs:

- A concurrency cap must not become the user-facing policy by itself; it is a safety rail, not the rate-limit strategy.
- Queueing can worsen event-loop pressure if unbounded. Prefer `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After`, or a very small bounded queue only if product requirements demand smoothing.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If there is no reliable client key, require one for public access or apply a global emergency limiter while accepting reduced fairness.
- If premium client tiers exist, bucket capacity and refill rate should be configurable by tier.

## Seat 003 - Pragmatic Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium

Recommendation: implement the simplest per-client token bucket in memory, return `429` with `Retry-After` when empty, and skip a full sliding-window implementation.

Rationale:

- Token bucket is the smallest strategy that directly matches the stated behavior: allow normal bursts, slow down sustained import batches.
- In-memory state is acceptable for the given single-process constraint and avoids introducing Redis or a gateway dependency before it is needed.
- The endpoint is a slug utility, so a clear reject-and-retry contract is easier to operate than a server-side queue.
- Fixed-window counters are simpler but less fair and create burst cliffs.
- Concurrency-only protection is simpler but does not answer the caller fairness requirement.

Trade-offs:

- Configuration should start conservative and be adjusted with observed traffic. A reasonable first pass is capacity 50, refill 10 tokens per second per client, TTL eviction after 10 to 15 minutes idle, and a separate low global concurrency cap sized to the process.
- Client key extraction must be explicit and documented. Do not silently group all users behind one proxy IP unless that is the intended policy.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If small callers are ever throttled, increase capacity before increasing refill rate.
- If batch clients still pin the event loop, lower the concurrency cap or move heavy work off the request path.

1: # Multi-AI Council Report: Rate-Limit Strategy for the Slug API
2: 
3: ## Task Classification
4: 
5: Planning-only architecture decision for a single-process Node HTTP endpoint exposing `POST /slugify`. No implementation changes were made. External AI systems were not invoked; all seats are simulated in-CLI reasoning lenses, labeled accordingly.
6: 
7: ## Council Composition
8: 
9: | Seat | Strategy Lens | AI Vantage Target | Distinct Mandate | Confidence |
10: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
11: | seat-001 | Analytical Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high | Select the strategy that best satisfies burst tolerance, single-process constraints, and graceful degradation. | 86 |
12: | seat-002 | Critical Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh | Attack failure modes: missing client identity, batch abuse, memory growth, fairness, and event-loop exhaustion. | 82 |
13: | seat-003 | Pragmatic Seat | simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium | Minimize operational complexity while protecting common callers and avoiding an external store. | 84 |
14: 
15: ## Seat 001 - Analytical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high
16: 
17: Recommendation: use a per-client token bucket with a burst allowance as the primary rate limiter.
18: 
19: Rationale:
20: 
21: - The common caller sends fewer than 10 requests per burst, so the bucket capacity should be comfortably above that, such as 30 to 50 tokens per client.
22: - Batch clients that fan out thousands of titles in seconds consume the burst budget quickly, then degrade predictably through refill rate and `Retry-After` responses.
23: - A token bucket fits a single-process Node service with an in-memory map keyed by client identifier and TTL eviction for inactive buckets.
24: - Compared with a sliding-window log, it avoids storing one timestamp per request during batch spikes.
25: - Compared with fixed-window counters, it avoids hard boundary bursts where a caller can double-dip across adjacent windows.
26: 
27: Trade-offs:
28: 
29: - Per-client identity is required for fairness. If no client key exists, the plan should not pretend to be per-client; a temporary global bucket is only a coarse protective fallback.
30: - Token refill math and clock handling are slightly more complex than fixed-window counters, but the complexity is still small and bounded.
31: 
32: Conditions that would change the recommendation:
33: 
34: - If the service scales to multiple processes or hosts, in-memory buckets become inconsistent and the design should move to a shared store or gateway-level limiter.
35: - If each slugify call becomes CPU-expensive, add a stricter concurrency cap in front of the handler.
36: 
37: ## Seat 002 - Critical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh
38: 
39: Recommendation: do not rely on rate alone; pair a per-client token bucket with a small in-flight concurrency cap.
40: 
41: Rationale:
42: 
43: - The incident symptom is event-loop exhaustion under batch clients. A token bucket slows future arrivals but does not by itself cap work already admitted during a burst.
44: - A concurrency cap protects the event loop from too many simultaneous slugify calls, especially if request parsing or future transformations become heavier than the toy utility.
45: - Sliding-window logs are precise but invite memory growth under exactly the pathological client behavior described.
46: - Fixed-window counters are easy to bypass at window boundaries and may punish callers unlucky enough to land near a boundary.
47: - A pure concurrency limit is insufficient because it lets a batch client keep the service permanently saturated as soon as slots free up.
48: 
49: Trade-offs:
50: 
51: - A concurrency cap must not become the user-facing policy by itself; it is a safety rail, not the rate-limit strategy.
52: - Queueing can worsen event-loop pressure if unbounded. Prefer `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After`, or a very small bounded queue only if product requirements demand smoothing.
53: 
54: Conditions that would change the recommendation:
55: 
56: - If there is no reliable client key, require one for public access or apply a global emergency limiter while accepting reduced fairness.
57: - If premium client tiers exist, bucket capacity and refill rate should be configurable by tier.
58: 
59: ## Seat 003 - Pragmatic Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium
60: 
61: Recommendation: implement the simplest per-client token bucket in memory, return `429` with `Retry-After` when empty, and skip a full sliding-window implementation.
62: 
63: Rationale:
64: 
65: - Token bucket is the smallest strategy that directly matches the stated behavior: allow normal bursts, slow down sustained import batches.
66: - In-memory state is acceptable for the given single-process constraint and avoids introducing Redis or a gateway dependency before it is needed.
67: - The endpoint is a slug utility, so a clear reject-and-retry contract is easier to operate than a server-side queue.
68: - Fixed-window counters are simpler but less fair and create burst cliffs.
69: - Concurrency-only protection is simpler but does not answer the caller fairness requirement.
70: 
71: Trade-offs:
72: 
73: - Configuration should start conservative and be adjusted with observed traffic. A reasonable first pass is capacity 50, refill 10 tokens per second per client, TTL eviction after 10 to 15 minutes idle, and a separate low global concurrency cap sized to the process.
74: - Client key extraction must be explicit and documented. Do not silently group all users behind one proxy IP unless that is the intended policy.
75: 
76: Conditions that would change the recommendation:
77: 
78: - If small callers are ever throttled, increase capacity before increasing refill rate.
79: - If batch clients still pin the event loop, lower the concurrency cap or move heavy work off the request path.

## Seat 001 - Analytical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 high

Recommendation: use a per-client token bucket with a burst allowance as the primary rate limiter.

Rationale:

- The common caller sends fewer than 10 requests per burst, so the bucket capacity should be comfortably above that, such as 30 to 50 tokens per client.
- Batch clients that fan out thousands of titles in seconds consume the burst budget quickly, then degrade predictably through refill rate and `Retry-After` responses.
- A token bucket fits a single-process Node service with an in-memory map keyed by client identifier and TTL eviction for inactive buckets.
- Compared with a sliding-window log, it avoids storing one timestamp per request during batch spikes.
- Compared with fixed-window counters, it avoids hard boundary bursts where a caller can double-dip across adjacent windows.

Trade-offs:

- Per-client identity is required for fairness. If no client key exists, the plan should not pretend to be per-client; a temporary global bucket is only a coarse protective fallback.
- Token refill math and clock handling are slightly more complex than fixed-window counters, but the complexity is still small and bounded.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If the service scales to multiple processes or hosts, in-memory buckets become inconsistent and the design should move to a shared store or gateway-level limiter.
- If each slugify call becomes CPU-expensive, add a stricter concurrency cap in front of the handler.

## Seat 002 - Critical Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 xhigh

Recommendation: do not rely on rate alone; pair a per-client token bucket with a small in-flight concurrency cap.

Rationale:

- The incident symptom is event-loop exhaustion under batch clients. A token bucket slows future arrivals but does not by itself cap work already admitted during a burst.
- A concurrency cap protects the event loop from too many simultaneous slugify calls, especially if request parsing or future transformations become heavier than the toy utility.
- Sliding-window logs are precise but invite memory growth under exactly the pathological client behavior described.
- Fixed-window counters are easy to bypass at window boundaries and may punish callers unlucky enough to land near a boundary.
- A pure concurrency limit is insufficient because it lets a batch client keep the service permanently saturated as soon as slots free up.

Trade-offs:

- A concurrency cap must not become the user-facing policy by itself; it is a safety rail, not the rate-limit strategy.
- Queueing can worsen event-loop pressure if unbounded. Prefer `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After`, or a very small bounded queue only if product requirements demand smoothing.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If there is no reliable client key, require one for public access or apply a global emergency limiter while accepting reduced fairness.
- If premium client tiers exist, bucket capacity and refill rate should be configurable by tier.

## Seat 003 - Pragmatic Seat / simulated cli-opencode gpt-5.5 medium

Recommendation: implement the simplest per-client token bucket in memory, return `429` with `Retry-After` when empty, and skip a full sliding-window implementation.

Rationale:

- Token bucket is the smallest strategy that directly matches the stated behavior: allow normal bursts, slow down sustained import batches.
- In-memory state is acceptable for the given single-process constraint and avoids introducing Redis or a gateway dependency before it is needed.
- The endpoint is a slug utility, so a clear reject-and-retry contract is easier to operate than a server-side queue.
- Fixed-window counters are simpler but less fair and create burst cliffs.
- Concurrency-only protection is simpler but does not answer the caller fairness requirement.

Trade-offs:

- Configuration should start conservative and be adjusted with observed traffic. A reasonable first pass is capacity 50, refill 10 tokens per second per client, TTL eviction after 10 to 15 minutes idle, and a separate low global concurrency cap sized to the process.
- Client key extraction must be explicit and documented. Do not silently group all users behind one proxy IP unless that is the intended policy.

Conditions that would change the recommendation:

- If small callers are ever throttled, increase capacity before increasing refill rate.
- If batch clients still pin the event loop, lower the concurrency cap or move heavy work off the request path.
